summary:
The establishment of Zoroastrianism as the state religion of the [[event:2059|Sassanid Empire]] (from the 3rd century AD) represented the culmination and codification of an ancient Iranian religious tradition — transforming a faith that had existed for centuries as the dominant but diffuse religion of the Iranian world into an institutionalized state church with a professional priesthood (the Magi/Zoroastrian clergy), a canonical text (the Avesta, compiled and systematized under Sassanid patronage), and official enforcement. The Sassanid kings bore the title 'King of Kings of the Aryans' and the Zoroastrian fire temples dotted the empire as symbols of royal legitimacy.

Zoroastrianism, founded by the prophet Zarathustra (Zoroaster) sometime between 1500 and 600 BC, was a profoundly influential faith whose concepts — a supreme deity (Ahura Mazda), a cosmic struggle between good and evil, a final judgment of souls, bodily resurrection, and apocalyptic end times — significantly influenced Judaism during the Babylonian exile, and through Judaism influenced Christianity and Islam. Under the Sassanids, Zoroastrian priests held enormous social and political power; religious minorities (Jews, Christians, Manichaeans) faced periodic persecution, though conditions varied significantly by ruler. The Arab Muslim conquest of the 7th century ended Sassanid power and gradually marginalized Zoroastrianism in Iran — many Zoroastrians fled to India, where their descendants (the Parsis) maintain the tradition today. Zoroastrianism remains a living religion with perhaps 100,000–200,000 adherents worldwide.
